content.text: The Purpose of Review Cadence

Setting OKRs at the start of a quarter without a structured review cadence produces the same outcome as not setting them: teams discover shortfalls too late to act, and the end-of-quarter score is a post-mortem rather than a management tool. Review cadence converts OKRs from a planning artefact into an active operating mechanism.
The value of an OKR review is not the meeting — it is the corrective action that follows. A review that produces no decisions or owner-assigned actions is overhead, not governance.
The Four Review Points

Four structured review points in a 13-week quarter balance early-warning capability against meeting overhead. The weekly automated refresh provides continuous visibility without human effort; the four check-ins add judgment and decision-making at the right moments.
Weekly Automated Refresh

The weekly automated job pulls current values from each KR's designated data source, computes RAG status (comparing progress to a linear time-proportional expectation), and publishes to the team dashboard. No human involvement unless a KR transitions to RED.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class RAGStatus(Enum):
    GREEN    = "green"
    AMBER    = "amber"
    RED      = "red"
    ACHIEVED = "achieved"

@dataclass
class WeeklyKRSnapshot:
    kr_id: str
    week_ending: date
    current_value: float
    target_value: float
    expected_value: float    # linear progress expectation at this week
    rag: RAGStatus
    delta_vs_expected: float  # positive = ahead of pace

def compute_expected_value(
    baseline: float,
    target: float,
    week_number: int,
    total_weeks: int = 13,
) -> float:
    """Linear interpolation from baseline at W0 to target at W13."""
    progress_fraction = week_number / total_weeks
    return baseline + (target - baseline) * progress_fraction

def compute_rag(
    current: float,
    target: float,
    expected: float,
    direction: str = "higher_is_better",
) -> RAGStatus:
    if direction == "higher_is_better":
        if current >= target:
            return RAGStatus.ACHIEVED
        gap = expected - current        # positive = lagging
        if gap <= 0:
            return RAGStatus.GREEN
        gap_pct = gap / target
        return RAGStatus.AMBER if gap_pct <= 0.15 else RAGStatus.RED
    else:  # lower_is_better
        if current <= target:
            return RAGStatus.ACHIEVED
        gap = current - expected        # positive = lagging (worse than expected)
        if gap <= 0:
            return RAGStatus.GREEN
        gap_pct = gap / target
        return RAGStatus.AMBER if gap_pct <= 0.15 else RAGStatus.RED

def auto_refresh(
    kr_id: str,
    baseline: float,
    target: float,
    current_value: float,
    week_number: int,
    direction: str = "higher_is_better",
) -> WeeklyKRSnapshot:
    expected = compute_expected_value(baseline, target, week_number)
    rag = compute_rag(current_value, target, expected, direction)
    delta = (current_value - expected) if direction == "higher_is_better" else (expected - current_value)
    return WeeklyKRSnapshot(
        kr_id=kr_id,
        week_ending=date.today(),
        current_value=current_value,
        target_value=target,
        expected_value=expected,
        rag=rag,
        delta_vs_expected=round(delta, 4),
    )
Week 2 Kickoff Check-In

The kickoff check-in (Week 2) confirms the operational plumbing before the quarter is in full swing. It catches setup problems early — a KR whose data source is not yet configured, a dependency that is already blocked, or an owner who does not know they are responsible for a KR.
Kickoff checklist
Cascade alignment
: run the alignment check; confirm every team KR maps to a programme objective.
Data source confirmed
: for each KR, the owner confirms the data source is live and returning values. A KR with no data source by W2 is at risk.
Baseline values locked
: record the W1 value for each KR. This is the true baseline; the target is set relative to this, not a hypothetical starting point.
Owner acknowledgement
: each KR owner confirms they understand the target, the measurement method, and the expectation for their W5 pulse update.
Blocking dependencies
: cross-team dependencies are reviewed; any blocker not yet resolved is escalated to the programme lead immediately.
Week 5 Pulse Check

The pulse check (Week 5) is intentionally lightweight — async, 15 minutes maximum per team. Its purpose is early detection of KRs that are already falling behind pace, not problem-solving. Problem-solving happens offline or at the W9 session.
Pulse check format
Each team provides a one-line status update per KR in a shared document or channel:
Week 9 Course-Correction Session

The W9 session is the most important review of the quarter. At this point, four weeks remain — enough time to make a material difference to at-risk KRs, but not enough time to recover a badly lagging one without a realistic assessment. The session has two purposes: root-cause at-risk and off-track KRs, and decide whether adjustments to targets are warranted.
Running the W9 session
Open with data
: display the current RAG dashboard — no verbal recaps. Every participant reviews the data in the 2 minutes before discussion begins.
Triage RED KRs only
: AMBER KRs are managed by their owners between now and W13. RED KRs get group attention. For each RED: root cause, owner of corrective action, due date.
Target adjustment decision
: if a RED KR's root cause is external (vendor delay, data unavailability, scope change) rather than execution failure, the committee may adjust the target. Document the reason.
Write the log
: every decision made in the session is logged with a named owner and due date. The log is shared within 24 hours of the session.

@dataclass
class CourseCorrectAction:
    kr_id: str
    root_cause: str
    corrective_action: str
    owner: str
    due_date: date
    target_adjusted: bool = False
    new_target_value: Optional[float] = None
    adjustment_reason: Optional[str] = None   # required if target_adjusted=True

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        issues = []
        if self.target_adjusted and self.new_target_value is None:
            issues.append("new_target_value required when target_adjusted=True")
        if self.target_adjusted and not self.adjustment_reason:
            issues.append("adjustment_reason required when target_adjusted=True")
        return issues

ADJUSTMENT_VALID_REASONS = [
    "external_vendor_delay",
    "data_unavailability",
    "scope_change_approved",
    "resource_reduction",
    "market_condition_change",
]
# Execution failure is NOT a valid adjustment reason — targets should be missed
# and learned from, not quietly lowered to make the quarter look better.
Quarter-Close Scoring and Retrospective
The W13 close converts the quarter's OKR journey into a learning input for the next quarter. Two deliverables are produced: a scored card for every KR and a retrospective document for missed KRs.

@dataclass
class QuarterCloseResult:
    quarter: str
    kr_id: str
    final_value: float
    target_value: float
    score: float                       # final_value / target_value, capped at 1.0
    grade: str                         # "green" (>=0.7), "amber" (>=0.4), "red" (<0.4)
    was_target_adjusted: bool
    target_adjusted_reason: Optional[str]
    retrospective_note: str            # required for score < 0.7

    @classmethod
    def compute(
        cls,
        quarter: str,
        kr_id: str,
        final_value: float,
        target_value: float,
        adjusted: bool = False,
        adjustment_reason: Optional[str] = None,
        retro_note: str = "",
    ) -> "QuarterCloseResult":
        score = min(final_value / target_value, 1.0) if target_value else 0.0
        grade = "green" if score >= 0.7 else "amber" if score >= 0.4 else "red"
        return cls(
            quarter=quarter, kr_id=kr_id, final_value=final_value,
            target_value=target_value, score=round(score, 2), grade=grade,
            was_target_adjusted=adjusted, target_adjusted_reason=adjustment_reason,
            retrospective_note=retro_note,
        )
Target Adjustment Policy

Target adjustments mid-quarter are the most sensitive decision in the OKR process. Done correctly, they reflect honest re-planning; done incorrectly, they are grade inflation that destroys the credibility of the OKR system.
Scenario
Decision
Rationale
Vendor API delayed → KR data unavailable
Adjust target or mark KR as blocked
External dependency; not execution failure
Team underestimated effort → behind pace
No adjustment; miss the target; learn from it
Adjusting here rewards poor planning
Business priority shifted → KR no longer relevant
Cancel the KR; replace with a relevant one
Better to close honestly than score a moot metric
Scope expanded beyond original KR definition
Adjust target upward proportionally
Fairer than crediting over-delivery on a re-scoped KR
Early market feedback shows target was wrong
Adjust with documented evidence
Market data is a valid external input
Dashboard Design for Review Meetings

The review sessions are only as good as the data in front of participants. A purpose-built OKR dashboard for review meetings should surface four things instantly: current RAG per KR, trend over the last 4 weeks, time elapsed vs progress made, and corrective actions from prior sessions with their due dates.

@dataclass
class OKRDashboardRow:
    kr_id: str
    kr_description: str
    owner_role: str
    current_value: float
    target_value: float
    progress_pct: float
    time_elapsed_pct: float     # week_number / 13
    rag: RAGStatus
    prior_rag: RAGStatus        # last week's status (for trend indicator)
    open_action_count: int      # corrective actions not yet resolved

    @property
    def trend(self) -> str:
        if self.rag.value < self.prior_rag.value:
            return "improving"
        if self.rag.value > self.prior_rag.value:
            return "deteriorating"
        return "stable"

    def pace_summary(self) -> str:
        expected = self.time_elapsed_pct
        actual = self.progress_pct
        gap_pct = round((actual - expected) * 100, 1)
        direction = "ahead of" if gap_pct >= 0 else "behind"
        return f"{abs(gap_pct)} % {direction} pace"

def build_review_dashboard(
    rows: list[OKRDashboardRow],
    week_number: int,
) -> str:
    """Render a simple text table for async distribution before the live session."""
    header = f"OKR Dashboard — Week {week_number}"
    separator = "─" * 90
    lines = [header, separator,
             f"{'KR':<30} {'Owner':<20} {'Progress':>10} {'RAG':<8} {'Trend':<14} {'Actions':>7}",
             separator]
    for r in sorted(rows, key=lambda x: x.rag.value, reverse=True):
        lines.append(
            f"{r.kr_id:<30} {r.owner_role:<20} "
            f"{r.pace_summary():>10} {r.rag.value.upper():<8} "
            f"{r.trend:<14} {r.open_action_count:>7}"
        )
    lines.append(separator)
    red = sum(1 for r in rows if r.rag == RAGStatus.RED)
    amber = sum(1 for r in rows if r.rag == RAGStatus.AMBER)
    lines.append(f"Summary: {len(rows)} KRs — {red} RED, {amber} AMBER, {len(rows)-red-amber} GREEN/ACHIEVED")
    return "\n".join(lines)
Common Cadence Failures
Skipping the W5 pulse
: teams discover at W9 that a KR has been lagging since W3. The correction window is now too short.
W9 as status report, not decision session
: discussing what the data shows without assigning corrective action owners. The meeting produces no outputs.
Adjusting targets to avoid red scores
: the team learns that targets are movable when execution lags. Next quarter's targets will be set lower from the start.
No retrospective for missed KRs
: the same root causes recur in Q+1 because they were never documented and addressed at close.
Automated refresh not connected to actual data
: KR current values are manually updated, introducing lag and optimism bias. Wire every KR to an automated source by W2.
Summary

OKR review cadence for a GenAI programme uses four structured review points across a 13-week quarter: kickoff (W2) to confirm alignment and baseline; pulse (W5) for lightweight async status; course-correction (W9) for root-cause and corrective action on RED KRs; and quarter-close (W13) for final scoring and retrospective. A weekly automated refresh provides continuous RAG visibility without human overhead. Target adjustments mid-quarter are permitted only for external-dependency failures — not execution shortfalls — and require documented reasons. Retrospectives for missed KRs are mandatory, not optional, because they are the primary mechanism by which the programme improves its planning accuracy over time.
